title: Examining Scaling and Transfer of Language Model Architectures for Machine Translation

structured.summary: This paper presents a systematic study comparing language model-based architectures (PrefixLM and CausalLM) with traditional encoder-decoder Transformers for machine translation across various languages, scales, and transfer settings. It finds that while encoder-decoder models generally excel in supervised bilingual and multilingual translation, PrefixLM models show superior zero-shot performance by reducing off-target translations, and that architectural differences diminish as model size grows.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical comparison across multiple architectures and scales
strengths.evidence: The paper evaluates PrefixLM, CausalLM, and encoder-decoder models on bilingual, multilingual, and zero-shot translation tasks, varying model depth and width.

strengths.point: Insightful findings on scaling behavior and masking schemes
strengths.evidence: Results show that full-visible source masking (PrefixLM) outperforms causal masking (CausalLM), and that architectural performance gaps narrow as model size increases.

strengths.point: Demonstration of improved zero-shot translation with PrefixLM
strengths.evidence: PrefixLM models outperform encoder-decoder models in zero-shot BLEU and reduce off-target translation, indicating better cross-lingual transfer.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient engagement with prior work on prefix and causal LMs in MT
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks explicit comparison or referencing of related architectures like Song et al. (2019) or T5 models that employ prefix LM approaches.
No ablation studies on intermediate masking schemes within PrefixLM variants or detailed rationale for TopOnly variant design choice.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation and reproducibility details are incomplete
weaknesses.evidence: Main reliance on BLEU metric without other quality assessments; missing explicit confirmation of identical data preprocessing; lack of hyperparameter and vocabulary details.

weaknesses.point: Efficiency analysis lacks real-world training/inference measurements
weaknesses.evidence: Only FLOPs are reported without wall-clock time or memory usage, limiting practical deployment insights.

weaknesses.point: Limited linguistic analysis and language diversity
weaknesses.evidence: Zero-shot improvements are not linked to linguistic phenomena, and experiments focus mainly on English-centric high-resource languages.

weaknesses.point: Presentation lacks statistical significance or variance reporting
weaknesses.evidence: Reported BLEU improvements do not include confidence intervals or results over multiple runs.

questions.question: How would hybrid or mixed masking strategies between full-visible and causal masking affect translation quality?
questions.motivation: Exploring intermediate designs could balance efficiency and inductive bias, potentially improving performance.

questions.question: Can the reported architectural findings and scaling laws generalize beyond Transformer models to other sequence models?
questions.motivation: Understanding generality would broaden applicability of conclusions to diverse MT architectures.

questions.question: What are the effects of architectural choices under domain shift, noisy data, or truly low-resource language conditions?
questions.motivation: Examining robustness would inform practical deployment in varied real-world scenarios.

questions.question: How do differences in tokenization, vocabulary sharing, and training hyperparameters impact the fairness and reproducibility of the comparisons?
questions.motivation: Clarifying these factors is critical for replicability and interpreting performance differences.

critique_points.point_001: Insufficient engagement with prior work on prefix and causal LMs in MT. The paper lacks explicit comparison or referencing of related architectures like Song et al. (2019) or T5 models that employ prefix LM approaches
critique_points.point_002: Limited methodological exploration and justification. No ablation studies on intermediate masking schemes within PrefixLM variants or detailed rationale for TopOnly variant design choice
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation and reproducibility details are incomplete. Main reliance on BLEU metric without other quality assessments; missing explicit confirmation of identical data preprocessing; lack of hyperparameter and vocabulary details
critique_points.point_004: Efficiency analysis lacks real-world training/inference measurements. Only FLOPs are reported without wall-clock time or memory usage, limiting practical deployment insights
critique_points.point_005: Limited linguistic analysis and language diversity. Zero-shot improvements are not linked to linguistic phenomena, and experiments focus mainly on English-centric high-resource languages
critique_points.point_006: Presentation lacks statistical significance or variance reporting. Reported BLEU improvements do not include confidence intervals or results over multiple runs
